Ms. Jennifer Poelking
Committee to Elect Joe Cortez Sheriff 2010
828 North 5th Street
Grover Beach, CA 93433
Your Request for lnformal Assistance
Our File No. I-10-068
Dear Ms. Poelking:
This letter re'sponds to your request for informal assistance regarding campaign
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Please note that our advice is Uaiea solely
on provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other laws
such as Elections Code provisions.
You represent candidate Joe Coftez who is running for Sheriff in 2010, San Luis Obispo
County, California. You state that the Cortez campaign wants to send 2,000 or more postcard
mailers to voters. You wanted to find a way to get people to hold on to the card and to reinforce
the candidate's theme of giving back to the community. You have a "Wanted" theme since it is
a sheriff race. To add to this theme you wanted to offer a reward with a charity twist.
You state that you plan to have the postcards numbered by the printing company. people
would be told (on the card) to look in the local newspaper on election day, Juie 8th,'for'an ad to
see if they have the matching number. They will also be told on the maiier that if they do have
the correct number, the $1,000 reward will be donated in the name of the voter to the charity of
his or her choice. You state that the charitable donation may be given out of the candidate's own
' The Political Refbrm Act is contained in Government Code sections 8 I 000 through g l0 I 4. All sratutory
ref'erences are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the piir political practices
Commission are contained in Sections l8 I l0 through I 8997 of Title 2 of thl California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, r.rnless otherwise indicate6. In
addition, because your request does not present a question under the Act, we cannot provide formal advice, but are
providing informalassistance that dcres not provide the requestor with immunitl. (Rigulatien 13329(b)(3XD) and
(c).)
an


File No. I-10-068
Page No. 2
funds.2 The card would tell the voter how the candidate has and will give back as sheriff. The
candidate will draw the number at random from a hat and all of the numbers will be included.
As I discussed with the candidate by phone, there are no provisions of the Act that would
prohibit the campaign from sending the postcard mailer you desCribed. There are, however,
provisions in the California Elections Code which prohibit a person from giving a gift, payment
or other consideration to induce a person to vote or refrain from voting. 6lections Code
Sections 1852I and 18522.) Because the provisions prohibiting induciments for voting are
outside the Act, the Fair Political Practices Commission does not have the authority tolnterpret
those statutes. These Elections Code provisions are interpreted by the Attorney General's Office
and the Secretary of State. We urge you to contact those agenciei on this quesiion.
The Act, of course, requires that all campaign mailings sent by a candidate have proper
sender identification. (Section 84305.) The name of the candidate, his qr her street address, city,
state, and zip code must be placed on the outside of each piece of mail in no less than 6-point
type and in a color or print which contrasts with the background so that it is easily read. If, as
here, a single candidate's controlled committee is sending a mailer, the name of the committee
which contains the candidate's name may be placed on the outside of the mailer. A post office
box may be used as the address only if the committee's street address is on its statement of
organization on file with the Secretary of State.
We are sorry that we cannot be of more assistance on this issue. If you have questions on
other matters, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Hyla P. Wagner
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

charity, the candidate should deposit the funds in the campaign bank account and write the check to thJclarity tiom
that account, because the_expenditure is campaign related. (Section 85201.) Campaign funds are permitted to be
used fbr charitable donations. (Section 895 15.)

sections.question: Whether a campaign can send 2,000+ postcard mailers to voters with a promotional contest offering a $1,000 reward to be donated to a charity of the voter's choice if their numbered postcard matches a number published in a local newspaper on election day.
sections.conclusion: There are no provisions of the Act that would prohibit the campaign from sending the postcard mailer described. However, California Elections Code Sections 18521 and 18522 prohibit giving gifts, payments or other considerations to induce voting, which are outside the FPPC's authority. The campaign must comply with proper sender identification requirements under Section 84305 (candidate name, street address, city, state, zip code in no less than 6-point contrasting type on the outside of each mailer).
sections.question_synthetic: Whether the Political Reform Act permits a candidate campaign to conduct a numbered postcard mailer promotion offering charitable donations as prizes to encourage voter retention of campaign materials.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC found no Political Reform Act violations with the proposed postcard mailer, but referred the requestor to the Attorney General's Office and Secretary of State regarding Elections Code provisions that may prohibit inducements for voting. The campaign must include proper sender identification on all mailers.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Informal advice letter (note indicates formal advice not provided due to lack of question under the Act). Document clearly structured with question and conclusion sections despite parsing notes stating no section headers found. Footnotes contain regulatory interpretation guidance.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a campaign can send 2,000+ postcard mailers to voters with a promotional contest offering a $1,000 reward to be donated to a charity of the voter's choice if their numbered postcard matches a number published in a local newspaper on election day.

CONCLUSION: There are no provisions of the Act that would prohibit the campaign from sending the postcard mailer described. However, California Elections Code Sections 18521 and 18522 prohibit giving gifts, payments or other considerations to induce voting, which are outside the FPPC's authority. The campaign must comply with proper sender identification requirements under Section 84305 (candidate name, street address, city, state, zip code in no less than 6-point contrasting type on the outside of each mailer).

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises a campaign for a sheriff candidate that while the Political Reform Act does not prohibit a numbered postcard mailer promotion offering charitable donations as prizes, Elections Code provisions prohibiting voting inducements may apply and should be reviewed with the Attorney General's Office and Secretary of State. Proper campaign sender identification is required on all mailers.
